title: Se présenter
description: Introducing yourself: name, age, nationality, profession. Uses être + avoir from Lesson 04.

cards.notes: Reflexive verb. The *m'* is the elided form of *me* (myself). *Je m'appelle Marie* — I call myself Marie.

cards.back: I am … years old. (lit. *I have … years*)
cards.notes: French uses *avoir* for age. Never *Je suis 25 ans* — that's the English calque most learners produce first.
*à* + city: *J'habite à Paris*. For countries it's *en* (feminine) or *au* (masculine): *en France*, *au Canada*.
Lowercase as an adjective: *Je suis français*. Capitalized only as a noun: *un Français* (a French person).
Add **-e** for feminine: *Je suis française*. Most nationalities follow this pattern.
Note: *anglais* covers England specifically. For "British" use *britannique* (invariable — same form m/f).
When stating a profession with *être*, French DROPS the article: *Je suis étudiant* — not *un étudiant*.
Traditionally invariable: *un professeur* for any gender. *Une professeure* (with an extra **-e**) is increasingly accepted but still feels new.
Add the silent **-e** in writing if you're feminine: *Enchantée*. Sounds the same either way.

steps.title: The four pieces of an introduction
steps.body:
# Introducing yourself in French

A full A1 introduction has four pieces, and you can do all four with verbs you already know from Lesson 04 (*être* and *avoir*).

1. **Name** — *Je m'appelle ___* (I call myself ___)
2. **Age** — *J'ai ___ ans* (I have ___ years)
3. **Nationality** — *Je suis ___* (I am ___)
4. **Profession** — *Je suis ___* (I am ___)

End with *Enchanté(e)* — "nice to meet you."

Most everyday introductions are shorter — just name + maybe profession. But knowing the full pattern means you can also UNDERSTAND it when someone introduces themselves to you.

steps.title: Saying your name
steps.body:
## Je m'appelle

This is a **reflexive** verb: *s'appeler* — to call oneself.

- *Je **m'**appelle Marie.* — My name is Marie.
- *Tu **t'**appelles comment ?* — What's your name? (informal)
- *Vous **vous** appelez comment ?* — What's your name? (formal/plural)
- *Il / Elle **s'**appelle…* — His / her name is…

The *me / te / se* forms agree with the subject and elide before a vowel (*m'appelle*, *t'appelles*, *s'appelle*).

At A1, you really only need the first-person form: **Je m'appelle ___**. Memorise it as one chunk; the grammar will click later.

steps.title: Saying your age — with *avoir*
steps.body:
## J'ai … ans

French uses **avoir** (to have) for age, never *être*. Literally you say *I have twenty-five years*.

- *J'**ai** vingt-cinq ans.* — I am 25 years old.
- *Tu **as** quel âge ?* — How old are you? (informal)
- *Vous **avez** quel âge ?* — How old are you? (formal)
- *Elle **a** trente ans.* — She is 30.

Numbers 1-10 came in Lesson 02. Numbers 20-100 follow patterns but aren't drilled here; for now, just know the construction: *J'ai [number] ans*.

exercise.prompt: Match each French phrase with its English translation.

steps.title: Nationality
steps.body:
## Saying your nationality

Use **être** + adjective. The adjective agrees with you (m. or f.).

| English | m. | f. |
|---|---|---|
| French | français | française |
| German | allemand | allemande |
| Spanish | espagnol | espagnole |
| English | anglais | anglaise |
| Italian | italien | italienne |

Most nationalities add **-e** for feminine. A few double the final consonant (italien → italienne, canadien → canadienne).

**Lowercase** as an adjective: *Je suis française*. **Capitalized** only as a noun: *un Français habite ici* (a French person lives here).

exercise.prompt: A woman saying she's German says…

steps.title: Profession — no article!
steps.body:
## Saying what you do

With **être** + a profession, French **drops the article**:

- *Je suis **étudiant**.* — I am a student. (NOT *un étudiant*)
- *Elle est **médecin**.* — She is a doctor.
- *Tu es **professeur** ?* — Are you a teacher?

This is one of the few times French is more compact than English. Don't add *un* / *une* / *des* unless you're describing the person further:

- *Je suis un bon étudiant.* — I am a good student. (description → article reappears)
- *C'est un médecin célèbre.* — He's a famous doctor.

exercise.prompt: A woman saying *I am a student* writes:
exercise.hint: Profession after *être* — drop the article. Feminine form adds -e.

exercise.prompt: Arrange to mean: *My name is Paul and I am French.*
Match each question to what it asks about.
Someone has just introduced themselves. As a man, you reply:
exercise.hint: One word. The masculine form (no extra -e in writing).
